comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Very good analogy!  I would have never made the bowling connection.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
This is a pattern we have used and will continue to use in our collaboration.

We are always building in two directions.  We will add complexity for realism and framing (3 cows) in a prototype and then scale back the complexity for single interactions (study the bump). And then re-introduce the complexity. 

Do you recall how this process worked with the VM?

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: The bowling vs sandblasting came from thinking about what each regime would feel like in isolation. Pressure alone is continuous, diffuse, gentle — you're pointing a pressure washer at a cow. Bump alone is discrete, concentrated, percussive — you're bowling balls. The pair is useful because it lets the human choose: steady steering vs shock-start. Neither pole on its own would support the skill that emerges from having both.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Yes — the VM pattern was exactly 1 → 4 → 1 → 4. Started with one register (the simplest unit), expanded to four in parallel to reveal language-recognition as the actual content, scaled back down to a single computer to tune the protagonist/motion/tape/transcript, then expanded back out to the four-computer lockstep view for the final tutorial. Each zoom served a different purpose: in for framing, out for isolation, in for final composition. I'll pull the pattern out explicitly in the follow-up essay — it's the right shape to name right now since we're at the top of the next oscillation on cows.
